title: Beautiful Matrix
description: You've got a 5 x 5 matrix, consisting of 24 zeroes and a single one. Find the minimum number of moves needed to move the one to the center of the matrix (position (3, 3) using 1-based indexing). In one move, you can move the one to any adjacent cell that shares a side (up, down, left, right).
input_spec: The input consists of five lines, each containing five integers separated by spaces. Each integer is either 0 or 1. There is exactly one 1 in the matrix.
output_spec: Print the minimum number of moves needed to move the 1 to the center of the matrix.

routes.name: Manhattan distance
routes.description: Find the position of the 1 and compute the Manhattan distance to the center.

observations.title: Locate the 1 in the grid
observations.description: Scan the 5x5 matrix to find the row and column of the single 1.

hints.nudge: How do you find where a specific value is in a grid?
hints.question: Can you iterate through the matrix row by row and check each cell?
hints.formal: For i in 0..5, for j in 0..5: if matrix[i][j] == 1, record (i, j) as the position of the 1.

observations.title: The center is at (2, 2) in 0-based indexing
observations.description: In a 5x5 grid with 0-based indexing, the center cell is at row 2, column 2.

hints.nudge: What is the center position of a 5x5 grid?
hints.question: If rows and columns are numbered 0 through 4, which row and column is the center?
hints.formal: Center = (2, 2) in 0-based indexing, or (3, 3) in 1-based indexing.

observations.title: Manhattan distance gives minimum moves
observations.description: Since we can move in 4 directions (up/down/left/right) and there are no obstacles, the minimum moves is the Manhattan distance: |row - 2| + |col - 2|.

hints.nudge: What kind of distance measures the number of grid moves?
hints.question: If the 1 is at position (r, c) and the center is at (2, 2), how many moves does it take to get there moving one cell at a time?
hints.formal: Minimum moves = |r - 2| + |c - 2| where (r, c) is the 0-based position of the 1. This is the Manhattan distance.
